name: Lauryn Hill
description:
'''Lauryn Noelle Hill''' (born May 26, 1975) is an American rapper, singer, songwriter, record producer, and actress. Regarded as one of the most influential musicians of her time and considered a pioneer of [[neo soul]] and [[rap-singing]], she has been credited by ''[[The Telegraph (London)|The Telegraph]]'' with popularizing [[hip-hop music]]. In 2015, ''[[Billboard (magazine)|Billboard]]'' named her the [[Billboard and Vibe's 50 Greatest Rappers of All Time|greatest female rapper]]. Hill has been ranked one of ''[[Rolling Stone]]''<nowiki/>'s [[Rolling Stone's 200 Greatest Singers of All Time|200 Greatest Singers]], [[VH1]]'s [[The Greatest (VH1 TV series)|100 Women in Music]], and [[NPR]]'s [[50 Great Voices]]. She is also the [[List of highest-grossing live music artists#Rankings by category|highest-grossing American female rapper in live music]], and has won eight [[Grammy Awards]], the [[Grammy Award milestones|most for any female rapper]].

Hill began her career as a teen actress, portraying Kira Johnson in the [[soap opera]] ''[[As the World Turns]]'' (1991) before appearing in [[Steven Soderbergh]]'s film ''[[King of the Hill (1993 film)|King of the Hill]]'' (1993). She received acclaim for her role as Rita Watson in ''[[Sister Act 2: Back in the Habit]]'' (1993), which featured her rendition of "[[His Eye Is on the Sparrow]]". Hill achieved prominence as the frontwoman of the [[Fugees]]. Their second album, ''[[The Score (album)|The Score]]'' (1996), sold over 22 million copies worldwide, including the singles "[[Killing Me Softly with His Song|Killing Me Softly]]" and "[[Ready or Not (Fugees song)|Ready or Not]]". It won the Grammy Award for [[Grammy Award for Best Rap Album|Best Rap Album]], making Hill the first woman to receive the honor. 

Her debut solo album, ''[[The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill]]'' (1998), debuted atop the [[Billboard 200|''Billboard'' 200]] with the highest first-week sales for a female artist at the time and became the first recording by a female rapper to be certified [[List of music recording certifications|diamond]]. Its lead single "[[Doo Wop (That Thing)]]" debuted at number one on the [[Billboard Hot 100|''Billboard'' Hot 100]], making Hill the first artist whose first entries on both of those charts debuted at number one. The album produced several hits, including "[[Ex-Factor]]", "[[Everything Is Everything (Lauryn Hill song)|Everything Is Everything]]", and "[[Lost Ones (Lauryn Hill song)|Lost Ones]]". It remains one of the [[List of best-selling albums|best-selling albums]] worldwide and ranked atop [[Apple Music 100 Best Albums|Apple Music's 100 Best Albums]]. At the [[41st Annual Grammy Awards]], Hill became the first rapper to win [[Grammy Award for Album of the Year|Album of the Year]] and the first woman to win five awards in a single night. Afterwards, she embarked on [[The Miseducation Tour]] and became the first rapper to appear on the cover of [[Time (magazine)|''Time'']].

Around this time, Hill became a noted collaborator, featuring on "[[If I Ruled the World (Imagine That)]]" by [[Nas]] and "[[The Sweetest Thing (Refugee Camp All Stars song)|The Sweetest Thing]]" by [[Refugee Camp All-Stars]]. She produced recordings for [[Whitney Houston]], [[Mary J. Blige]], and [[Santana (band)|Santana]], wrote [[Aretha Franklin]]'s "[[A Rose Is Still a Rose (song)|A Rose Is Still a Rose]]", and released the [[Bob Marley]] duet "[[Turn Your Lights Down Low]]". Her 2002 live album ''[[MTV Unplugged No. 2.0]]'' developed a [[Cult following|cult status]], with "[[I Gotta Find Peace of Mind]]" and "[[Mystery of Iniquity]]" being sampled by other artists. In the years that followed, Hill made fewer public appearances while occasionally releasing songs such as "[[Black Rage (song)|Black Rage]]" and "[[Nobody (Nas and Lauryn Hill song)|Nobody]]".

Hill is the recipient of [[List of awards and nominations received by Lauryn Hill|numerous accolades]] including [[American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers|ASCAP]]'s Golden Note Award, the [[NAACP Image Award – President's Award|NAACP President's Award]], and inductions into the [[Grammy Hall of Fame]], the [[National Recording Registry]], and the [[Black Music & Entertainment Walk of Fame]]. In 2026, she was nominated for the [[Rock and Roll Hall of Fame]].
